title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 4. Kṛṣṇasandarbha
author: Jīva Gosvāmī
genre: philosophical-theological treatise (with commentary on the Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
summary: The fourth of Jīva Gosvāmī's six Sandarbhas (Ṣaṭsandarbha or Bhāgavata-sandarbha), a systematic exposition of Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava theology drawing heavily on the Bhāgavata Purāṇa. The Kṛṣṇasandarbha argues that Kṛṣṇa, not a mere avatāra, is the original and complete form of Bhagavān (svayaṃ bhagavān), and establishes him as the supreme object of loving devotion, treating his līlā, dhāman (Vraja), and associates.
history: Jīva Gosvāmī (c. 1513-1598), nephew of Rūpa and Sanātana Gosvāmī, systematized the theology of the Caitanya movement at Vrindavan; the Sandarbhas were partly based on notes of Gopāla Bhaṭṭa. They remain the doctrinal foundation of the Gauḍīya school and were commented upon by Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa.

title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 5. Bhaktisandarbha
author: Jīva Gosvāmī
genre: philosophical-theological treatise (with commentary on the Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
summary: The fifth of the Ṣaṭsandarbhas, devoted to bhakti (devotion). It establishes devotional service to Bhagavān as the sole direct means (abhidheya) to God-realization, analyzing the varieties and stages of bhakti, the qualifications of the devotee, the relation of bhakti to karma and jñāna, and the centrality of the holy name.
history: Composed by Jīva Gosvāmī at Vrindavan as part of his systematization of Gauḍīya theology, drawing primarily on the Bhāgavata Purāṇa. The Bhaktisandarbha is among the most studied of the six and was commented upon by Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa.

title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 6. Prītisandarbha
author: Jīva Gosvāmī
genre: philosophical-theological treatise (with commentary on the Bhāgavata Purāṇa)
summary: The sixth and final Sandarbha, treating prīti or prema-bhakti — pure love of God — as the supreme goal (prayojana) of human life. It analyzes the nature of divine love, its relation to liberation (which it surpasses), the rasa-theology of devotional emotion, and the eternal blissful relationship between the soul and Kṛṣṇa.
history: The culminating volume of Jīva Gosvāmī's Ṣaṭsandarbha, completing the structure of sambandha (relation), abhidheya (means) and prayojana (goal). It is foundational for Gauḍīya aesthetics of devotion and was commented upon by Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa.

title: Ajaḍapramātṛsiddhi
author: Utpaladeva
summary: A short philosophical work ('Proof of a Sentient/Non-inert Knower'), one of the three treatises of Utpaladeva's Siddhitrayī. It argues for the necessity of a conscious, self-luminous knowing subject, against Buddhist and other positions that would reduce cognition to insentient processes, and shows that the limited individual knower is ultimately grounded in the one unlimited Self, establishing the non-dual nature of reality.
history: Utpaladeva (c. 900-950 CE) was the chief originator of the Pratyabhijñā ('recognition') system of monistic Kashmir Śaivism, building on his teacher Somānanda and later developed by Abhinavagupta. The Siddhitrayī (Ajaḍapramātṛsiddhi, Īśvarasiddhi, Sambandhasiddhi) accompanies his major work the Īśvarapratyabhijñākārikā; the text survives with a Vṛtti.

title: Vaikhānasamantrapraśna
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the Vaikhānasa school of Vikhanas)
tradition: Hindu (Vaiṣṇava; Vaikhānasa Āgama tradition, rooted in the Taittirīya/Black Yajurveda)
date_estimate: early to mid-1st millennium CE (mantra layer archaic; disputed)
summary: The mantra-portion ('Mantrapraśna', in praśnas) of the Vaikhānasa corpus, containing the liturgical mantras used in Vaikhānasa Vaiṣṇava temple and domestic worship. It includes numerous accented mantras of clearly Vedic character not found in other Yajurveda Saṃhitās, supplying formulae for the consecration and worship of Viṣṇu and associated deities.
history: The Vaikhānasas, followers of the sage Vikhanas, are the hereditary priests (arcakas) of more than half the Viṣṇu temples of South India, including Tirupati. Their tradition claims direct Vedic descent and is regarded as more orthodox/conservative than the Pāñcarātra. The Mantrapraśna is the mantra core complementing the Vaikhānasa Gṛhya/Smārta sūtras and Āgama saṃhitās; a critical edition/translation of Mantrapraśna V-VIII was published by Harvard University Press.

title: Viṣṇudharma
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 3rd-5th c. CE (disputed; an early Vaiṣṇava layer)
summary: An early Vaiṣṇava 'śāstra'/upapurāṇa (distinct from the larger encyclopedic Viṣṇudharmottara) cast as a dialogue, in the extant version comprising about 105 chapters in which the sage Pulastya (or Śaunaka) instructs Dālbhya on Vaiṣṇava dharma — vows, worship of Viṣṇu, devotional observances and the merit of devotion to Viṣṇu.
history: Studied by R. C. Hazra among the Upapurāṇas, the Viṣṇudharma is regarded as one of the older Vaiṣṇava texts, its composition linked to the spread of Buddhism and 'heretical' faiths in the centuries after Aśoka. It is to be distinguished from the encyclopedic Viṣṇudharmottara Purāṇa, of which it is the conceptual predecessor; a critical edition was prepared by R. Grünendahl.

title: Viṣvaksenasaṃhitā
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed revelation by Viṣvaksena)
summary: A Pāñcarātra Āgama scripture of roughly 2800 verses in 39 chapters, dealing with image-worship and temple ritual: the theology and consecration of pratimā icons, iconographic prescriptions, and the construction of the prāsāda (temple structure) to house the images. It is classed as a sāttvika text of the 'Muniprokta' group of Pāñcarātra saṃhitās.
history: Named for Viṣvaksena, Viṣṇu's commander and gate-guardian, this saṃhitā belongs to the Pāñcarātra Āgama corpus that governs Vaiṣṇava temple liturgy in South India. Its precise date and authorship are unknown, as is typical of the Pāñcarātra revelatory literature; it is cited in later ritual digests.

title: Catuḥślokī
author: Yāmuna (Yāmunācārya / Āḷavandār)
summary: A short devotional hymn of four verses ('Four Stanzas') in praise of Śrī (Lakṣmī), extolling the goddess's supreme role as the consort of Nārāyaṇa and as the mediatrix who grants grace and liberation to individual souls (jīvas). It is foundational for Śrī Vaiṣṇava theology of Lakṣmī's salvific function.
history: Yāmunācārya (traditionally c. 918-1038 CE), grandson of Nāthamuni and predecessor of Rāmānuja, was a key systematizer of Viśiṣṭādvaita. The Catuḥślokī is one of his six works and, with his Stotraratna, established the devotional tone of the tradition that Rāmānuja later elaborated philosophically.

title: Stotraratna
author: Yāmuna (Yāmunācārya / Āḷavandār)
summary: The 'Jewel of Hymns', a celebrated devotional poem in praise of Nārāyaṇa (Viṣṇu). It expresses self-surrender (prapatti) and the soul's complete dependence on God's grace, blending intense bhakti with the doctrinal framework of Viśiṣṭādvaita, and opens with homage to the lineage of teachers (Nāthamuni, etc.).
history: Composed by Yāmunācārya (c. 918-1038 CE), the Stotraratna deeply influenced Rāmānuja, who is traditionally said to have been moved to take up the teacher's mission upon Yāmuna's death. It remains a core liturgical and devotional text of the Śrī Vaiṣṇava community.

title: Kāśikāvṛtti
author: Jayāditya and Vāmana
summary: A running commentary (vṛtti) on Pāṇini's Aṣṭādhyāyī, the oldest surviving complete commentary covering all of Pāṇini's nearly 4000 sūtras in their original sequence. Noted for lucid explanations, illustrative examples, and awareness of earlier grammatical works (Mahābhāṣya, Cāndravyākaraṇa), it makes Pāṇini's cryptic rules accessible.
history: Traditionally co-authored by Jayāditya and Vāmana (c. 7th century CE), reportedly with Jayāditya responsible for the first five adhyāyas and Vāmana the rest. It became the standard reference for Pāṇinian study and was itself the basis of the major sub-commentaries Nyāsa (Jinendrabuddhi) and Padamañjarī (Haradatta).

title: Aṣṭādhyāyī
author: Pāṇini
date_estimate: c. 5th-4th c. BCE (commonly c. 350 BCE; disputed)
summary: The foundational grammar of Sanskrit, comprising nearly 4000 terse, algebraically organized sūtras in eight chapters (aṣṭa-adhyāyī) that generate correct Sanskrit forms through an ordered system of rules, metarules, and technical conventions. It is the most comprehensive and rigorous descriptive grammar of antiquity and effectively standardized Classical Sanskrit.
history: Composed by Pāṇini, probably in the northwest (Śalātura, Gandhāra) around the mid-1st millennium BCE, drawing on earlier grammarians it names. Its rule system is often compared to a formal generative grammar. It was elaborated by Kātyāyana's Vārttikas and Patañjali's Mahābhāṣya, and remains the bedrock of the entire Sanskrit grammatical tradition.

title: Aṣṭādhyāyī
author: Pāṇini
date_estimate: c. 5th-4th c. BCE (commonly c. 350 BCE; disputed)
summary: The foundational grammar of Sanskrit in nearly 4000 sūtras across eight chapters, generating correct word-forms through an ordered, rule-governed system with sophisticated metalinguistic conventions. (This is a second digital recension/encoding of the same work as GS24panini_u.)
history: By Pāṇini (mid-1st millennium BCE). The work standardized Classical Sanskrit and was elaborated by Kātyāyana and Patañjali; this entry appears to be a separate electronic edition of the same text within the corpus.

title: Paribhāṣenduśekhara
author: Nāgeśa Bhaṭṭa (Nāgojibhaṭṭa)
summary: The authoritative treatise on the paribhāṣās — the interpretive metarules governing the application and ordering of Pāṇini's sūtras. It collects, explains, and critically discusses these governing principles with rigorous argumentation, becoming the standard reference on the subject.
history: Written by Nāgeśa Bhaṭṭa (Nāgojibhaṭṭa), a Maharashtrian Brahmin grammarian active at Benares/Śṛṅgaverapura in the early 1700s under the patronage of the chieftain Rāma of the Bisena family. The most prolific late grammarian, his Paribhāṣenduśekhara attracted more than two dozen commentaries; it was edited and translated by F. Kielhorn.

title: Vyākaraṇamahābhāṣya [Mahābhāṣya]
author: Patañjali
date_estimate: c. 150 BCE (original; project hint 2nd-3rd c. CE may reflect a later recension)
summary: The 'Great Commentary' on Pāṇini's grammar, structured as a discursive commentary on Kātyāyana's Vārttikas and selected Pāṇinian sūtras (extant on 1228 of the 3981 sūtras). Beyond grammar it ranges into philosophy of language, semantics, and methodology, often in lively dialogue form, and is the principal source for Kātyāyana's otherwise lost Vārttikas.
history: Attributed to Patañjali and conventionally dated to c. 150 BCE on the strength of internal references (e.g., to Yavana/Greek campaigns and Puṣyamitra's sacrifice). The third of the 'trimuni' (three sages: Pāṇini, Kātyāyana, Patañjali), it is the supreme authority of the grammatical tradition; the project's not_before/not_after (100-299 CE) likely reflects the dating of a particular transmitted recension rather than original composition.

title: Samanvayadiś
author: Kuḍaka
summary: A Sanskrit work attributed to an author named Kuḍaka; the title ('Samanvayadiś', roughly 'the direction/method of syntactic concord or connection') suggests a treatise on samanvaya — the construal or syntactic-semantic connection of words in a sentence. Could not be confidently identified or its contents verified from available reference sources.
history: Neither the author Kuḍaka nor the work appears in standard reference catalogues or the consulted databases; it may be a minor or modern grammatical/linguistic composition preserved in a digital corpus (e.g., GRETIL). Identification is uncertain and details could not be corroborated.

title: Vākyapadīya 1.1-3.7
author: Bhartṛhari
summary: A portion (kāṇḍa 1 through chapter 7 of kāṇḍa 3) of Bhartṛhari's seminal treatise on the philosophy of language and grammar. The work expounds Śabdabrahman (the Word as ultimate reality), the sphoṭa theory of meaning-bearing linguistic units, and the relation of sentence and word to meaning; book 3 (Padakāṇḍa) treats semantic categories such as substance, action, time, gender, and number.
history: Bhartṛhari (c. 5th century CE) is the foundational thinker of the grammarian school of Indian philosophy (Vaiyākaraṇa-darśana). The Vākyapadīya (also called Trikāṇḍī) integrates Pāṇinian grammar with metaphysics and deeply influenced later linguistics, philosophy, and aesthetics; this entry covers a defined segment of the text.

title: Vākyapadīya
author: Bhartṛhari
summary: Bhartṛhari's foundational treatise (also Trikāṇḍī) on the philosophy of language in three books (kāṇḍas): the Brahmakāṇḍa on Śabdabrahman and the metaphysics of language, the Vākyakāṇḍa on the sentence and sphoṭa, and the Padakāṇḍa (Prakīrṇakāṇḍa) on individual words and semantic categories. (This entry appears to be the kāṇḍa-1 / full-text encoding of the same work as GS24vakyp_pu.)
history: Bhartṛhari (c. 5th c. CE) founded the grammarian-philosophical school, fusing Pāṇinian linguistics with non-dual metaphysics centered on language. The Vākyapadīya was commented upon (e.g. the Vṛtti, Puṇyarāja, Helārāja) and profoundly shaped Indian theories of meaning and aesthetics.

title: Laghusiddhāntakaumudī [Laghukaumudī]
author: Varadarāja
summary: A widely used introductory manual of Pāṇinian grammar that abridges Bhaṭṭoji Dīkṣita's Siddhāntakaumudī, rearranging Pāṇini's sūtras topically and presenting only the most essential rules (the laghu 'short' version reduces them dramatically) so that beginners can master core Sanskrit grammar without the full system's complexity.
history: Composed by Varadarāja, a 17th-century pupil of Bhaṭṭoji Dīkṣita, who produced three graded abridgements (madhya, laghu, sāra) of his teacher's Siddhāntakaumudī. The Laghukaumudī remains the standard teaching text in traditional pāṭhaśālās and in modern courses on Pāṇinian grammar.

title: Nāmaliṅgānuśāsana [Amarakoṣa, Amarakośa], Kāṇḍa 1
author: Amarasiṃha
summary: The first kāṇḍa of the Amarakoṣa ('Immortal Treasury'), the most famous Sanskrit thesaurus, a versified synonym-dictionary arranged thematically. Kāṇḍa 1 (the 'celestial/divine' division) covers vargas on heaven, the directions and sky, time, intellect/sound, drama, the netherworld, hell, and the waters — grouping synonyms by subject with notes on grammatical gender.
history: Composed by Amarasiṃha, traditionally said to be a Buddhist and a 'jewel' of a royal court (often legendarily linked to Vikramāditya), around the middle of the first millennium CE. Memorized for centuries as the standard Sanskrit lexicon, it set the model for later kośas and attracted more than forty commentaries (e.g. Kṣīrasvāmin's Amarakośodghāṭana).

title: Nāmaliṅgānuśāsana [Amarakoṣa, Amarakośa], Kāṇḍa 2
author: Amarasiṃha
summary: The second and largest kāṇḍa of the Amarakoṣa, the 'earthly/terrestrial' division (Bhūmivarga, etc.). It groups synonyms by subject across the natural and social world — earth, towns, mountains, plants, animals, humans, the four varṇas and occupational classes, warriors, and the like — serving as a thematic dictionary with gender notation.
history: Part of Amarasiṃha's Nāmaliṅgānuśāsana (c. mid-1st millennium CE), the standard Sanskrit thesaurus memorized throughout traditional education. Its subject-wise arrangement became the template for nearly all later Sanskrit lexicons and generated an extensive commentarial literature.
